pages.text: Preliminary analysis of concrete reinforced with waste bottles PET ﬁbers Dora Foti ⇑ Technical University of Bari, Faculty of Engineering, Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Via Orabona, 4-70125 BARI, Italy article info Article history: Received 5 August 2010 Received in revised form 12 October 2010 Accepted 13 November 2010 Available online 21 December 2010 Keywords: Fiber-reinforced concrete PET bottles Characterization tests Ductility abstract As part of the research on ﬁber-reinforced concrete, the results of some tests for an approach to a broader testing on the possibility of using ﬁbers from polyethylene terephthalate (PET) bottles to increase the ductility of the concrete are reported. The ﬁbers are simply cut from waste plastic bottles reducing, in this way, the manufacturing costs of recycled PET ﬁber-reinforced concrete. The aim of this paper is to explore the possibility of re-cycling a waste material that is now produced in large quantities, while achieving an improvement of the ductility of the concrete. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Fiber-reinforced concrete is a composite material resulting from the addition of reinforcing ﬁbers to the brittle matrix of ordinary concrete. The idea of using ﬁber reinforcement comes from the need to ﬁnd a remedy to the phenomena of cracking, such as pro- duced by shrinkage, which inevitably affects the service life of con- crete buildings. Concrete is a material that is well resistant to compression, but has low tensile strength. The low level of tensile strength is caused by phenomena such as plastic and/or hydraulic shrinkage with the formation of unwanted micro and macro cracks. In addition to traditional measures and precautions to limit un- wanted shrinkage cracks such as keeping moist the surfaces of the coating in the early hours of maturation and by using speciﬁc additives, the use of reinforcing ﬁbers to stop or at least limit the propagation of cracks has been considered [1]. Fiber-reinforced concrete (FRC), in fact, performs better than normal concrete thanks to the ‘‘sewing effect’’ that the ﬁbers have on the cracks, with consequent beneﬁts in terms of toughness, ductility, impact strength (toughness), abrasion and fatigue. It has been noted experimentally that the ﬁbers are more effective in the post-crack phase, preventing the spread of cracks. Thus the practical beneﬁt achieved by the use of a ﬁbrous reinforcement is essentially a more widespread ductility of the structure in FRC and a reduced spread of shrinkage cracks. The aspect ratio of the ﬁbers must be correlated with the strength of those that have to be controlled, depending on the type of matrix used (mortar or concrete), to avoid the risk of reducing the workability of the mix that will determine the maximum value of resistance that the composite system can develop. In fact, for low doses (up to 2% of the total weight of concrete), used mainly for concrete, the ﬁbers provide an increase of toughness that is shown by the presence of a residual resistance after cracking has occurred, even if the behavior is degrading (softening) [2]. Increas- ing the percentage of ﬁbers (more than 2%) or their content by vol- ume, the structural response changes and shows hardening in the load–deﬂection curve ( Fig. 1 ). Synthetic ﬁbers show most success in practical applications and experiments since they show that they have qualities that other ﬁ- bers do not, such as: – they are chemi

impact resistance is improved, since a single crack cannot propa- gate freely through the element, but is prevented in this by the ﬁ- bers that form a 3-dimensional mesh reinforcement, intercepting fractures; there is also a signiﬁcant resistance to abrasion and chip- ping. Toughness and ductility of the composite are enhanced through the sewing effect that the ﬁbers have on the cracks, either by withdrawal or, more prominently, resulting from external loads. Toughness is a peculiar characteristic of FRC; it is deﬁned as the en- ergy absorbed prior to complete failure that, for a specimen in or- dinary concrete, happens suddenly in an uncontrolled fashion, while in the FRC it is gradual. The increased ductility is clearly shown by the fact that the collapse of a FRC element is not unex- pected, but it is predicted by a phase of large deformations associ- ated to a ﬁber elongation at break, which must be two or three orders of magnitude greater than the rupture deformation of the matrix, so that the collapse of the matrix will occur before the rup- ture of the ﬁbers. In the present paper the mechanical properties and behavior of concrete reinforced with polyethylene terephthalate (PET) ﬁbers obtained from waste bottles is studied. Different shapes of PET ﬁ- bers are utilized, strips and ‘‘O’’ ﬁbers. Comparisons of the behavior of specimens with and without ﬁbers during bending tests have been analyzed. Studies on PET ﬁbers have been developed in [4]. Among other properties, they show a sufﬁcient alkali resistance compared to other ﬁbers and this result makes PET ﬁbers good as concrete-rein- Fig. 1. Stress–strain diagram of ﬁber-reinforced concrete with different amounts of ﬁber. Fig. 2. (a) Sample of short lamellar ﬁbers and (b) sample of ‘‘O’’ﬁbers. Mixtures Concrete Portland (kg) Aggregate (kg) Fibers (g) Water (l) 1 Ordinary concrete 4 20 – 2.8 2 Concrete with short lamellar ﬁbers 4 20 50 2.8 3 Concrete with ‘‘O’’ ﬁbers 4 20 50 2.8 Fig. 3. Specimens for the ﬁrst series of preliminary tests. D. Foti / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 1906–1915 1907

forcing ﬁbers. Studies on concrete reinforced with PET ﬁbers have been previously developed [4,5], where the ﬁbers have been ex- truded to produce monoﬁlaments, or the surfaces of the ﬁbers have been coated with maleic anhydride grafted polypropylene and then chopped into the desired ﬁber length. These long manufactur- ing processes cause a reducing advantage in terms of costs. Fig. 4. (a) Instron electromechanical test machine, (b) test scheme and (c) placement of the specimen in the test machine. 1908 D. Foti / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 1906–1915

In this study PET ﬁbers have been obtained in a more simple way, just cutting waste bottles; in this way elaborate and costly manufacturing procedures have been avoided. The present re- search aims, in fact, to explore the possibility of using ﬁbers made from plastic bottles in the simplest and most economic way. It is therefore part of the research on re-cycling of a waste material (plastic bottles) that is produced in large quantities and difﬁcult to destroy [5]. This work belongs to the complex and ever-growing research in the ﬁeld of ﬁber-reinforced concrete, but the main aim is to show what impact the use of a ﬁbrous reinforcement within the concrete produces. The ﬁbers, evenly dispersed in the mixture to form a three- dimensional diffuse concrete reinforcement, are generally able to effectively distribute the usual internal and external stresses acting on the concrete. The analysis of the results put in evidence the effective increase of residual post-cracking strengths that qualify recycled PET ﬁber- reinforced concrete as an innovative material on which deepen the research with the aim to promote ever best performance in the construction ﬁeld. 2. Experimental analysis The experimental investigation conducted at the laboratory ‘‘M. Salvati’’ of the Technical University of Bari, had as main purpose to check the inﬂuence and efﬁcacy of a concrete ﬁber reinforcement adding PET ﬁbers, obtained from common plastic bottles of mineral water. Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) belongs to the family of poly- ester and it is a thermoplastic resin composed of phthalates suit- able primarily for food use. The elastic modulus of PET is E PET = 3100 N/mm2 that is lower than the concrete matrix one. Two series of tests have been performed: a ﬁrst series of very preliminary tests with a unique dosage of different ﬁbers, then a second series of tests with a common basic concrete matrix com- mercially available to keep the standard for a better comparison of the behavior of the same concrete with different dosages and typologies of ﬁbers. 2.1. First series of tests In the ﬁrst phase only very preliminary tests have been per- formed to check if the idea could give interesting results. With this aim, therefore, the specimens have been realized without exactly following the code prescriptions that is providing them with a cen- tral cut for bending tests. The specimens have been realized as sim- ple 100 /C2100 /C2400 mm parallelepipeds. The ordinary concrete was obtained by mixing composite Port- land concrete (Type II/A-L; 42.5 R) with gravel and sand of different sizes, and water. The same mix was utilized for the ﬁber-reinforced concrete, with the addition of the ﬁbers. Fibers were used both lamellar type shown in Fig. 2 a, with a length of 32 mm and a section of 2 /C20.1 mm 2, and circular ﬁbers (Fig. 2 b), with a diameter of 30–50 mm and a width variable around 5 mm, obtained, the latter ﬁbers, removing the ends of the 500 cc. bottles, crushing and cutting them normally to their longitudinal axis. Given that, from experiments carried out by other researchers [5], it was found that the introduction of ‘‘strips’’ of PET bottles in the concrete in place of sand, may determine, above certain quantities, a marked reduction in compression resis- tance of the concrete. For this reason, in this series of preliminary tests a very small quantity of PET ﬁbers, of the order of 0.26% of the concrete weight has been added for both the lamellar and ‘‘O’’ ﬁ- bers.

The three mixtures had in common the dosages of the various constituent materials, including ﬁbers, as shown in Table 2 . Composite Portland concrete of Type II/A-LL (SN EN 197- 1:2000) was utilized; it is called Portland Cement limestone, and contains 65–79% of clinker, 21–35% of lime and 0–5% of secondary components. For each of the three mixtures 20 kg of aggregates of various sizes were added, that is normal sand (0.10 mm size granules) and crushed stone. The samples were carefully prepared and stored for aging, in accordance with UNI EN 12390:2009. The fresh mixture was then poured into steel molds, which have been previously oiled with a product that facilitates the subse- quent disarmament. The samples of fresh concrete thus obtained were appropriately compacted and when this phase was com- pleted, the upper surface of the specimens were accurately leveled. Subsequently sheets of wet paper were placed on the molds to avoid a rapid loss of heat produced by hydration. The molds were stored for a period of 28 days at a temperature of 20 /C176±2/C176C and humidity (RH) of 90% as required by the code prescriptions. Fig. 3 shows a photo of the six specimens ready for the charac- terization tests. Subsequently, the six specimens were subjected to bending tests with a concentrated load at the centerline in order to verify their behavior. The bending tests were performed by an Instron electromechanical test machine of 50 kN maximum load, using the Wavemaker software Instron Ltd. Thus, it was possible to set all the test parameters, to view in real-time the load–displace- ment curves, to read, moment by moment, the values of load and displacement, to store and/or print the results of each test. Fig. 6. Load–displacement diagrams of the tested specimens. 1910 D. Foti / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 1906–1915

Fig. 4a–c shows, respectively, the test machine, the test scheme, and the geometry and constraints of the specimens and the place- ment of the specimens in the test machine to ensure the symmetry axis of the load. The test was divided into three steps to avoid differences in the test conditions to get for each specimen more comparable results: – Step 1: a constant gradient load test in which the crossbar was moved up at a steady rate to reach 0.5 kN at a time of 25 s. – Step 2: a constant gradient displacement test, during which the machine produced a constant speed of the movement of the crossbar of 0.5 mm/min. – Step 3: a constant gradient displacement test during which the machine produced a constant speed of the movement of the crossbar of 3 mm/min in the post-cracking phase. Visually it was possible to appreciate the sewing effect of the ﬁ- bers on the cracks induced by the load; this effect also reﬂected in the development of quantitative data to obtain the test results (Fig. 5 ). The photos also point out a deviation of the cracks that do not have the typical V-shape down that appears in ordinary concrete specimens at failure; this effect was induced by its own ﬁbers. Fig. 5 shows the crack formed to achieve the maximum tensile strength of the concrete specimens reinforced with PET ﬁbers and highlights the ability of ﬁbers to trap micro and macro cracks thus limiting the opening. 2.2. Results of the ﬁrst series of tests The results processed by the software Wavemaker provided the load–displacement diagrams shown in Fig. 6 . In a ﬁrst phase of loading the samples remained intact and this behavior was due because the concrete, for low loads, shows a lin- ear elastic-like behavior that resists both to tensile and compres- sion stresses. Reached the peak value, corresponding to the ultimate tensile strength of the concrete, the ﬁrst crack started to appear. From the diagrams it is clear that the peak value of the resis- tance remained almost unchanged around 8 kN and, conversely, what characterizes the six specimens is the different post-cracking behavior, which is the branch of the curve to the right of the peak, at which the process of cracking has been triggered. The curves conﬁrm the brittle behavior of specimens 1 N and 2 N in ordinary concrete with no reinforcement ﬁbers; in fact, it Table 4 List of the specimens utilized for the second series of tests. Mixture Specimen Characteristics 1S Prism no. 1: 100 /C2100 /C2400 mm in ordinary concrete A 2S Prism no. 2: 100 /C2100 /C2400 mm in ordinary concrete 1C (0.75%) Prism no. 1: 100 /C2100 /C2400 mm in concrete reinforced with circular ‘‘O’’ ﬁbers with a dosage equal to 0.75% of the total weight of the specimen B 2C (0.75%) Prism no. 2: 100 /C2100 /C2400 mm in concrete reinforced with circular ‘‘O’’ ﬁbers with a dosage equal to 0.75% of the total weight of the specimen 1C (0.50%) Prism no. 1: 100 /C2100 /C2400 mm in concrete reinforced with circular ‘‘O’’ ﬁbers with a dosage equal to 0.50% of the total weight of the specimen C 2C (0.50%) Prism no. 2: 100 /C2100 /C2400 mm in concrete reinforced with circular ‘‘O’’ ﬁbers with a dosage equal to 0.50% of the total weight of the specimen 1FC Prism no. 1: 100 /C2100 /C2400 mm in concrete reinforced with short lamellar ﬁbers with a dosage equal to 0.50% of the total weight of the specimen D 2FC Prism no. 2: 100 /C2100 /C2400 mm in concrete reinforced with short lamellar ﬁbers with a dosage equal to 0.50% of the total weight of the specime

is observed that after the point of ﬁrst crack, the specimen cracks almost suddenly, with very small deformations. For ﬁber-reinforced concrete specimens, with increasing bend- ing, the failure progresses continuously but there was no collapse of the composite because while the concrete was broken, the load previously supported was transmitted to the ﬁbers. Synthetic ﬁbers in the matrix, in fact, start to express their con- tribution after reaching the point of maximum tensile strength, i.e. the peak of the diagram, determining within the mix, the so-called ‘‘diffusion effect’’: they behave as ‘‘stitches’’ preventing the spread of cracks in the concrete and delaying hence the collapse that, however, is sudden in specimens of ordinary concrete without ﬁbers. The ﬁber-reinforced specimens with short lamellar ﬁbers (1FC and 2FC) show a modest increase in toughness (corresponding to the area under the load–displacement curve), which is shown in macroscopic terms, as a slower crack process, which avoided the sudden collapse of the specimen. Specimens reinforced with ‘‘O’’ ﬁbers (1FO and 2FO) showed a higher increase of toughness, probably due to the particular shape of the ﬁbers, which have a higher aspect ratio, while the load peak is of the same order of magnitude as the other ﬁber-reinforced examples. The closed form of the ﬁber produces a better bonding inside of the concrete of the two sides of the crack and it deter- mines a higher slippage resistance if compared to short lamellar ﬁbers. Table 3 shows the values of tensile strength for the six speci- mens tested for bending. The highest values are obtained for the specimens reinforced with short lamellar ﬁbers (1FC and 2FC). Fig. 10. Force–displacement diagrams of specimens reinforced with ‘‘O’’ ﬁbers and lamellar ﬁbers (0.50% dosage). Fig. 8. Tensile test on the ﬁbers. Fig. 9. 2.3. Second series of tests As a consequence of the results obtained from the previous tests, it was considered appropriate to further investigation, mak- ing more specimens, with different and increased ﬁber content. It was also preferred to make the tests repeatable to get comparable results. With this aim the matrix utilized in the second series of tests, was a premixed material commercially available. The ‘‘O’’ ﬁbers have a 6 cm diameter and a width of about 5 mm. The short lamellar ﬁbers have a 5 mm width and a 35 mm length. In short, samples were made very similar to those used for the ﬁrst preliminary tests; they are shown in Table 4 . In total eight specimens were realized and tested, two for each ﬁber typology, that is with ‘‘O’’ ﬁbers and short lamellar ﬁbers. For the specimens with ‘‘O’’ ﬁbers, two different ﬁber quantities were added. This choice was based on the results obtained in the ﬁrst series of tests where specimens with ‘‘O’’ ﬁbers performed bet- ter than those with lamellar ﬁbers. All mix were composed by 30 kg of premixed material and 3.95 l of water. Mix B was added with 225 g of ﬁbers, while mix C and D contained 150 g of ﬁbers. Fig. 13. Force–displacement diagrams of specimens with no ﬁber reinforcement and reinforced with ‘‘O’’ ﬁbers (0.75% dosage). Fig. 14. Energy dissipated in each ﬁber reinforced specimen.
